definition: A theory pertaining to a company’s optimal capital structure; the optimal level of debt is found at the point where additional debt would cause the costs of financial distress to increase by a greater amount than the benefit of the additional tax shield.

A result indicating that the null hypothesis can be rejected; with reference to an estimated regression coefficient, frequently understood to mean a result indicating that the corresponding population regression coefficient is different from zero.
An emotional bias in which people do nothing (i.e., maintain the status quo) instead of making a change.
A common method of voting where each share represents one vote.
Bonds for which the coupon, be it fixed or floating, increases by specified margins at specified dates.
A type of dividend in which a company distributes additional shares of its common stock to shareholders instead of cash.
An exchange in which equity securities are traded. See exchanges .
An increase in the number of shares outstanding with a consequent decrease in share price, but no change to the company’s underlying fundamentals.
The downward pressure on the share price of stock as large blocks of shares are being sold on the open market.
An order in which a trader has specified a stop price condition. Also called stop-loss order .
See stop order .

A resource that is no longer economically valuable owing to changes in demand, regulations, or availability of substitutes—for example, a newly discovered oil well that will not be brought into production.
A long-term strategy that establishes target allocations for various asset classes and aims to optimize the balance between risk and reward by diversifying investments.

A procedure that first divides a population into subpopulations (strata) based on classification criteria and then randomly draws samples from each stratum in sizes proportional to that of each stratum in the population.
For yield measures on fixed-income instruments that assume payments are made on scheduled dates and ignore weekends and holidays.
A specific type of scenario analysis that estimates losses in rare and extremely unfavorable combinations of events or scenarios.
A market in which security prices reflect all public and private information.
Also known as the cyclically adjusted budget deficit. The deficit that would exist if the economy was at full employment (or full potential output).

Refers to the positioning of debt, which is issued by a legal entity, typically a holding company, which is separate and therefore one step removed from an operating company, which holds assets pledged under a security agreement to senior secured debtholders.
A broad category of securities that incorporate the features of debt instruments and one or more embedded derivatives designed to achieve a particular issuer or investor objective.
A class of unsecured debt that ranks below a firm’s senior unsecured obligations.

A form of internal credit enhancement that relies on creating more than one bond tranche and ordering the claim priorities for ownership or interest in an asset between the tranches. The ordering of the claim priorities is called a senior/subordinated structure, where the tranches of highest seniority are called senior, followed by subor-
dinated or junior tranches. Also called .
Lending to borrowers with lower credit quality, high DTI, and/or are loans with higher LTV, and include loans that are secured by second liens otherwise subordinated to other loans.

A measure of the total deviation between observed and estimated values of the dependent variable. It is calculated by subtracting each estimated value ​ ˆ Y ​ i ​​ ​ from its corresponding observed value ​​ Y ​ i ​​​, squaring each of these differences, and then summing all of these squared differences.

A measure of the explained variation in the dependent variable, calculated as the sum of the squared differences between the predicted value of the dependent variable, Y ̂ _i, based on the estimated regression line, and the mean of the dependent variable, Y ̅ .

A measure of the total variation in the dependent variable in a simple linear regression. It is calculated by subtracting the mean of the observed values ​​ _ Y ​ from each of the observed values ​​ Y ​ i ​, squaring each of these differences, and then summing all of these squared differences.
A cost that has already been incurred.
A type of machine learning in which the system attempts to learn to model relationships based on labeled training data.

In some jurisdictions, a corporation’s board of directors is formally composed of a supervisory board and a management board. The supervisory board appoints and oversees the management board and often includes representatives of employees and other non-shareholder stakeholders.

The sequence of processes involved in the creation and delivery of a physical product to the end customer, both within and external to a firm, regardless of whether those steps are performed by a single firm.
A typically unexpected disturbance to supply.

Relates to the inclusion of only current investment funds in a database. As such, the returns of funds that are no longer available in the marketplace (have been liquidated) are excluded from the database. Also see backfill bias .
A firm commitment involving a periodic exchange of cash flows.
An agreement between two parties to exchange a series of future cash flows.
A swap trading platform
accessed by multiple .
The fixed rate to be paid by the fixed-rate payer specified in a swap contract.
A group of lenders, typically made up of banks.
The combination of a synthetic long underlying position (i.e., a long forward and risk-free borrowing) and a purchased put on the underlying.
Risk that affects the entire market or economy; it cannot be avoided and is inherent in the overall market. Systematic risk is also known as non-diversifiable or market risk .
A procedure of selecting every k th member until reaching a sample of the desired size. The sample that results from this procedure should be approximately random.
Refers to risks supervisory authorities believe are likely to have broad impact across the financial market infrastructure and affect a wide swath of market participants.

A proactive strategy that adjusts asset class allocations within a portfolio based on short-term market trends, economic conditions, or valuation changes to capitalize on temporary market inefficiencies or opportunities to improve returns or manage risk more effectively.

Management’s desired proportions of debt and equity financing, usually stated on a book value basis or indirectly using a financial leverage metric, such as net or gross debt to EBITDA or credit rating.
A bank’s ability to determine the definition of inflation that they target, the rate of inflation that they target, and the horizon over which the target is to be achieved.
A measure of downside risk, calculated as the square root of the average of the squared deviations of observations below the target (also called target downside deviation ).
Taxes that a government levies on imported goods.
The amount at which an asset or liability is valued for tax purposes.

An aggregate of an entity’s income tax payable (or recoverable in the case of a tax benefit) and any changes in deferred tax assets and liabilities. It is essentially the income tax payable or recoverable if these had been determined based on accounting profit rather than taxable income.
The portion of an entity’s income that is subject to income taxes under the tax laws of its jurisdiction.
Temporary differences that result in a taxable amount in a future period when determining the taxable profit as the balance sheet item is recovered or settled.
A form of security analysis that uses price and volume data, often displayed graphically, in decision making.

A solicitation by a current or prospective shareholder to other shareholders to acquire a substantial percentage, including 100%, of shares at a specified price. This action is usually undertaken by a potential acquirer whose bid was rejected by the issuer’s board of directors, prompting the potential acquirer to appeal directly to shareholders.
Remaining time-to-maturity for a fixed-income instrument.
Repos with a maturity longer than one day.
Also known as the maturity structure of interest rates, refers to the difference in interest rates or benchmark yields by time-to-maturity.
The expected value of a share at the end of the investment horizon—in effect, the expected selling price. Also called terminal value .
The expected value of a share at the end of the investment horizon—in effect, the expected selling price.

term: Test of the mean of the differences
definition: A statistical test for differences based on paired observations drawn from samples that are dependent on each other.

Involves the use of computer programs to analyze and derive meaning typically from large, unstructured textor voice-based datasets, such as company filings, written reports, quarterly earnings calls, social media, email, internet postings, and surveys.
Known risks that evolve and expand over a period of time. Climate change, pattern migration, the rise of populist forces, and the ongoing threat of terrorism fall into this category.
Describes a distribution that has relatively less weight in the tails than the normal distribution (also called platykurtic).
A pricing approach that charges different prices to different buyers, commonly based on volume purchased.
Entities that support institutional investors by managing their investments in timberland by analyzing and acquiring suitable timberland holdings.
Structure of a securitization that allows for the redistribution of “prepayment risk” among bond classes by creating bond classes of different expected maturities.
The difference between an option’s premium and its intrinsic value.
The process by which the time value of an option declines toward zero as the option’s expiration date is approached.

The compound rate of growth of one unit of currency invested in a portfolio during a stated measurement period; a measure of investment performance that is not sensitive to the timing and amount of withdrawals or additions to the portfolio.
The process of representing ownership rights to physical assets on a blockchain or distributed ledger.
An investment selection approach that begins with consideration of macroeconomic conditions and then evaluates markets and industries based upon such conditions.

term: Total probability rule for expected value
definition: A rule explaining the expected value of a random variable in terms of expected values of the random variable conditional on mutually exclusive and exhaustive scenarios.
Measures the price appreciation, or percentage change in price of the securities in an index or portfolio, plus any income received over the period.
An index that reflects the price appreciation or percentage change in price of the constituent securities plus any income received since inception.
The difference between current assets and current liabilities.
The standard deviation of the differences between a portfolio’s returns and its benchmark’s returns; a synonym of active risk.
The standard deviation of the differences between a portfolio’s returns and its benchmarks returns. Also called tracking error .
When regional integration results in the replacement of higher cost domestic production by lower cost imports from other members.
When regional integration results in lower-cost imports from non-member countries being replaced with higher-cost imports from members.
A portion or division of a private company sold via either direct sale or auction to a strategic buyer interested in increasing the scale and scope of an existing business.
The date when the buyer and seller transfer consideration and securities.
Markets for traditional investments, which include all publicly traded debts and equities and shares in pooled investment vehicles that hold publicly traded debts and/or equities.
A grouping of securities within an issue with characteristics that vary from other tranches, such as different credit quality and seniority.
Welfare payments made through the social security system that exist to provide a basic minimum level of income for low-income households.
Economic and financial losses from the tran sition to a lower-carbon economy in response to climate change—for example, the abandonment of an oil well that is no longer economical.
US Treasury bonds with a principal that is adjusted for changes in the Consumer Price Index. TIPS are issued in 5-, 10-, and 30-year maturities.
A measure of risk-adjusted performance that relates a portfolio’s returns in excess of the risk-free rate to a portfolio’s beta.
A mean computed after excluding a stated small percentage of the lowest and highest observations.

A repurchase agreement in which the transacting parties agree to use a third-party agent that provides access to a larger collateral pool and multiple counterparties, as well as valuation and safekeeping of assets.
Measures on fixed-income instruments use actual payment dates, accounting for weekends and holidays. The true yield on an instrument is always lower than the street convention yield.

Calendar anomaly that stock market returns in January are significantly higher compared to the rest of the months of the year, with most of the abnormal returns reported during the first five trading days in January.
The theory that all investors regardless of taste, risk preferences, and initial wealth will hold a combination of two portfolios or funds: a risk-free asset and an optimal portfolio of risky assets.
A table of the frequency distribution of observations classified on the basis of two discrete variables. Also known as Contingency table .
The interest rate on a two-week repurchase agreement; may be used as a policy rate by a central bank.
The error of rejecting a true null hypothesis; a false positive.
The error of not rejecting a false null hypothesis; false negative.

Sources of bank credit that a bank can refuse to honor. Uncommitted credit lines are made up to a certain principal amount for a pre-determined maximum maturity, charging a market reference rate plus an issuer-specific spread on only the principal outstanding for the period of use.

When a machine learning model treats true parameters as if they are noise and is unable to recognize relationships in the training data, making the model more likely to fail to fully discover patterns that underlie the data.
The asset referred to in a .

A type of securities issue mechanism in which the investment bank guarantees the sale of the securities at an offering price that is negotiated with the issuer. Also known as firm commitment offering .

A liability account for money that has been collected for goods or services that have not yet been delivered; payment received in advance of providing a good or service. Also called deferred revenue or deferred income .
A distribution with a single value that is most frequently occurring.
The expression of revenues and costs on a per-unit basis.
A hybrid or blended loan structure combining different tranches of secured and unsecured debt into a single loan with a single, blended interest rate.
Without collateral; unsecured debt is backed only by cash flows of the issuer.
A type of depository receipt in which the foreign company whose shares are held by the depository has no involvement in the issuance of the receipts.
A type of machine learning in which the system tries to learn the structure of unlabeled data.
Tokens that provide services within a network, such as paying for services and network fees.
Instructions which indicate when the order may be filled.
Businesses that distribute a product and also handle more complex aspects of product installation, customization, service, or support.
A money measure of the minimum value of losses expected during a specified time period at a given level of probability.
The systems and processes in a firm that create value for its customers.
The product or service attributes valued by a firm’s target customer that lead those customers to prefer that firm’s offering.
Strategies that involve larger-scale redevelopment and repositioning of existing assets and that may allow the investor to earn a higher return compared with core-plus real estate strategies.
Pricing set primarily by reference to the value of the product or service to customers.
See value at risk .
The expected value (the probability-weighted average) of squared deviations from a random variable's expected value.
The expected value (the probability-weighted average) of squared deviations from a random variable's expected value.
The difference between current margin required and the current collateral price in a repurchase agreement.
A measure of the sensitivity of an option’s price to changes in the underlying’s volatility.
The pace at which geopolitical risk impacts an investor portfolio.
Private equity investment in a startup or early-stage company involving high risk and a high rate of failure.
A hedge fund that seeks to buy, optimize, and ultimately sell portfolio companies to generate profits. See private equity fund .
Private debt funding that provides venture capital backing to start-up or early-stage companies that may be generating little or negative cash flow.
To become unconditionally entitled to.
The day that an employee becomes unconditionally entitled to compensation.
The year in which a private capital fund makes its first investment.
The standard deviation of the continuously compounded returns on the underlying asset.
A mechanism that allows a designated party— such as another shareholder, a shareholder representative, or management—to vote on the shareholder’s behalf.
The power of shareholders to cast votes in corporate elections for directors and other matters submitted to a shareholder vote.
Debt feature or security granting the right to purchase additional common equity shares issued by the company at a predetermined price for a given period.
These represent the distribution order for cash flows and risk to different tranches in a financing structure.
The belief that security prices fully reflect all past market data, which refers to all historical price and volume trading information.

Rate calculated for a mortgage pass-through security by weighting the mortgage rate of each mortgage in the pool by the percentage of the outstanding mortgage balance relative to the outstanding amount of all the mortgages in the pool.

Calculated for a mortgage pass-through security by weighting the remaining number of months to maturity of each mortgage in the pool by the outstanding mortgage balance relative to the outstanding amount of all the mortgages in the pool.

A weighted average of the after-tax required rates of return on a company’s equity, debt, preferred stock, and other long-term financing sources, where the weights are the fraction of each source of financing in the company’s current market value or management’s target capital structure.
A mean computed after assigning a stated percentage of the lowest values equal to one specified low value and a stated percentage of the highest values equal to one specified high value.
Refers to a transaction that has not gone well, and the investment is likely to lose value. The private equity firm revises the value of its investment downward or liquidates the portfolio company.
A graphical depiction of yields-to-maturity of bonds from the same issuer across maturities.
The difference in yield-to-maturity between a bond and that of a another bond.
An internal rate of return on a fixed-income instrument’s cash flows assuming cash flows are received on scheduled dates and the bond is called at a certain call price and date.
Internal rate of return on a fixed-income instrument’s cash flows assuming all cash flows are received on their scheduled dates.
The lowest among a fixed-income instrument’s yields-to-call and yield-to-maturity. A commonly cited yield measure for fixed-rate callable bonds.
or zero-volatility spread is a constant yield spread for a bond over a government or swap curve.
Bonds that do not pay interest during their life. They are issued at a discount to par value and redeemed at par. Also called pure discount bond.
